problems.title: Video Recommendation System
problems.description: Build a video recommendation system that personalizes content for YouTube users, maximizing engagement while introducing diverse and fresh content — not just more of what users already watch.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content:
**Goal**: Build a video recommendation system that personalizes content for YouTube users. The main goal is to maximize user engagement by recommending videos they are likely to watch and enjoy — while also introducing new and diverse content, not just more of what users already watch.

**Example**: If someone watches cooking videos all day, the system shouldn't just give them more of the same. It should also suggest travel vlogs, tech reviews, or short films they might find interesting.

**Goals**:
• Personalized recommendations based on user behavior
• Increase user engagement (watch time, click-throughs, conversions)
• Promote fresh content and not just historical favorites

**Scale**: 1.3 billion users, 150 billion video views/month, 10% from recommendations = 15 billion recommendation views/month.

sections.title: Metrics Design & Requirements
sections.content:
**Offline Metrics** (used during model training/validation):
• Precision & recall — how accurate and complete are recommendations?
• Ranking loss — measures how well the system ranks relevant videos higher
• Log loss — captures the model's confidence in predictions

**Online Metrics** (measure actual user behavior in production):
• Click-through rate (CTR) — do users click the videos we recommend?
• Watch time — how long do they stay engaged?
• Conversion rate — do they subscribe, like, or share?
• A/B tests validate whether the model performs well in the real world

**Requirements**:
• Training: Frequent retraining (multiple times/day) — user interests shift rapidly, videos go viral in hours
• Inference: Response time < 200ms (ideally < 100ms) — users expect instant recommendations on homepage
• Exploration vs. Exploitation: Balance recommending familiar content (exploitation) with surprising new content (exploration). Too much exploitation = repetitive feed; too much exploration = irrelevant recommendations

sections.title: Multi-Stage Model Architecture
sections.content:
**Two-stage architecture** (common pattern in large-scale ML systems):

**Stage 1 — Candidate Generation**:
• Purpose: Generate ~1000 "somewhat relevant" videos from billions, fast
• Method: Matrix Factorization (collaborative filtering) — captures user taste similarity in user-video space. For large scale (Facebook, Google), use FAISS or Google ScaNN for low-latency ANN search
• Input: User watch history (video IDs, minutes_watched)
• Output: ~1000 candidate videos

**Stage 2 — Ranking Model**:
• Purpose: Score each candidate for watch probability, return top-K
• Model: Fully connected neural network with sigmoid activation (final layer outputs probability ∈ [0,1])
• Loss: Cross-entropy loss
• Feature table:
  - Watched video IDs → Video embedding
  - Historical search query → Text embedding
  - Location → Geolocation embedding
  - User age/gender → Normalization/Standardization
  - Time features → month, week_of_year, holiday, hour_of_day
• Training data: User watched history; ratio watched/not-watched ≈ 2/98 → downsample negatives

sections.title: Calculation & Estimation
sections.content:
**Data assumptions**:
• 15 billion positive (watched) labels/month, 750 billion negative labels/month
• ~100 features per row at 500 bytes → 800 billion rows/month
• Total: 500 × 800 × 10⁹ ≈ 0.4 Petabytes/month
• Keep last 6 months in data lake; archive older data in cold storage

**Scale**:
• 1.3 billion users
• 10 million recommendation requests per second at peak
• Each request ranks 1K–10K video candidates

sections.title: High-Level System Design
sections.content:
**Databases**:
• User Watched History — which videos each user watched over time
• Search Query DB — historical queries users searched
• User/Video DB — user profiles + video metadata
• User Historical Recommendations — past recommendations per user

**Training Pipeline**:
• Resampling component: Downsample negative samples to scale training
• Feature pipeline: High-throughput feature generation (Spark, EMR, or DataProc)
• Model Repos: S3 or equivalent for model storage; inference pulls latest model by timestamp

**Inference Flow**:
1. User requests homepage → App Server calls Candidate Generation Service
2. Candidate Generation returns ~1000 videos
3. Candidates passed to Ranking Service → estimates watch probability per video
4. Ranking Service returns sorted list → App Server shows top videos

**Challenges & Solutions**:
• Huge data size → Use 1–6 months of recent data
• Imbalanced data (2% positive) → Random negative downsampling
• High availability → Deploy in Docker containers + Kubernetes Pod Autoscaler

sections.title: Scale the Design
sections.content:
• Scale out (horizontal) multiple Application Servers → Load Balancers distribute traffic
• Scale out Candidate Generation Services and Ranking Services independently
• Deploy in Kubernetes Pods with Pod Autoscaler for automatic scaling
• Use Kube-proxy so Candidate Generation Service calls Ranking Service directly → reduces latency
• This microservice split allows: independent build/deploy/release, reduced memory per service, and independent scaling

sections.title: Follow-Up Questions
sections.content:
**Q: How do we adapt to user behavior changing over time?**
→ Multi-arm bandit; Bayesian Logistic Regression to update priors; different loss functions less sensitive to CTR

**Q: How do we handle the ranking model being under-explored?**
→ Introduce 2% random candidates in Ranking Service; 98% get sorted candidates

**Q: Benefits of splitting Candidate Generation and Ranking Services?**
→ Independent build/deploy, reduced memory per pod, independent horizontal scaling

**Q: How do you handle cold-start for new users?**
→ Use demographic features + popular content for new users; gradually incorporate watch history

keyQuestions: How do you balance exploration vs. exploitation in recommendations?
Why split into candidate generation + ranking instead of one model?
How do you handle the 2/98 positive/negative imbalance?
How do you ensure fresh content without degrading relevance?
What's your retraining strategy and how often?
How do you handle cold-start for new users with no history?
keyAnswers: Use epsilon-greedy or Thompson sampling: dedicate ~10% of recommendations to content outside the user's watch history. Track engagement signals (clicks, watch time) to learn what's worth exploiting vs. exploring.

Candidate generation (matrix factorization + ANN search) filters billions to ~1K candidates in milliseconds. Running expensive feature-rich models on billions of items is infeasible — ranking only applies to the pre-filtered set.

Downsample negatives to a manageable ratio (e.g., 1:5), then apply class weights to the loss, or use focal loss which penalizes easy negatives less. Correct predicted probabilities back to original scale.

Add a recency feature (hours_since_upload) and an engagement velocity signal to the ranking model. Apply a freshness multiplier and dedicate a fixed percentage of slots to content uploaded in the last 24 hours.

Retrain candidate generation weekly (stable long-term preferences) and the ranking model daily or multiple times per day (captures rapidly shifting trends). Use streaming embedding updates for hot new content.

Use demographic features (age, location, device) + trending content for the first session. Once a user clicks 3-5 videos, switch to collaborative filtering. Use session-based models that adapt in real time within a session.

problems.title: LinkedIn Feed Ranking System
problems.description: Design a personalized LinkedIn feed ranking system that maximizes long-term user engagement. Rank posts and activities such that the most engaging and relevant content appears at the top of each user's home feed.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content:
**Goal**: Design a personalized LinkedIn feed to maximize long-term user engagement, measured by Click Through Rate (CTR) — the ratio of clicks to feed impressions.

**Feed Activity Types**:
| Category | Example |
|----------|---------|
| Connection | Member follows member/company, joins group |
| Informational | Member/company shares article/picture/message |
| Profile | Member updates profile (picture, job change) |
| Opinion | Member likes/comments on articles, job changes |
| Site-Specific | Member endorses member |

**Key insight**: Different activity types have very different CTRs, which matters when building models and generating training data.

**Scale**: 300 million users; feed ranking must return within 50ms of the full 200ms request budget.

sections.title: Metrics Design & Requirements
sections.content:
**Offline Metrics**:
• Normalized Cross-Entropy (NCE): Predictive log-loss divided by cross-entropy of background CTR → insensitive to background CTR variations. Formula: NCE = [-1/N Σ((1+yᵢ)/2·log(pᵢ) + (1-yᵢ)/2·log(1-pᵢ))] / [-(p·log(p) + (1-p)·log(1-p))]
• AUC

**Online Metrics**:
• Conversion rate (ratio of clicks to feed shown) — offline metrics don't capture real user behavior in non-stationary environments

**Training Requirements**:
• Large distributed training (Logistic Regression in Spark, or ADMM)
• Incremental retraining multiple times/day to handle online data distribution shift
• High personalization support — different users have different tastes

**Inference Requirements**:
• 300 million users at scale
• Latency: Full pipeline must complete in 200ms; Feed Ranking must return in 50ms
• Data freshness: Must avoid showing repetitive activity; pipelines must run fast

sections.title: Feature Engineering & Model
sections.content:
**Feature Engineering**:
| Feature | Encoding |
|---------|----------|
| User profile (job, industry, demo) | Low cardinality: One-hot; High cardinality: Embedding |
| Connection strength between users | User embeddings + distance vector |
| Age of activity | Continuous feature or binned |
| Activity features (type, hashtag, media) | Activity embedding + similarity to user |
| Cross features | Combine multiple features |

**Training Data Collection Strategies**:
• Chronicle order ranking: Rank by time → collect click/not-click data (has serving bias: attention on first posts; data sparsity for rare activities like job changes)
• Random serving: Random order → bad UX, doesn't help with sparsity
• Feed Ranking algorithm: Rank top feeds + random permutation within top → provides exploration, best for model learning

**Model Selection**:
• Logistic Regression: Probabilistic sparse linear classifier — efficient with sparse features, works well at scale
• Distributed training: Logistic Regression in Spark or ADMM for large volume
• Deep learning: Fully connected layers + Sigmoid activation. Resample to handle CTR < 1% imbalance. Keep validation/test sets intact.

sections.title: System Design
sections.content:
**Training Pipeline**:
• Collect click/impression logs
• Feature pipeline processes features with high throughput (Parquet files, Spark)
• Periodic training scheduler retrains model multiple times/day
• Model stored in Model Repos (S3)

**Inference Flow**:
1. User opens LinkedIn → App Server receives request
2. Data pipeline pulls recent activities from friends/connections
3. Feature extraction from Feature Store (< 10ms lookup)
4. Feed Ranking Model scores each activity → estimated CTR per post
5. Top-K posts returned within 50ms

**Key Components**:
• Feature Store: Redis or DynamoDB for low-latency (<10ms) feature lookup
• Data Freshness Tracker: Tracks which activities user has already seen → prevents repetition
• Personalization Layer: Per-user embeddings capture individual tastes

sections.title: Scaling & Follow-Up Questions
sections.content:
**Scale**:
• Horizontal scaling of App Servers + Load Balancers
• Distributed feature computation via Spark/EMR
• Separate ranking pods per user segment

**Follow-up Questions**:
**Q: How do you handle the data sparsity problem for rare activities (job changes)?**
→ Use activity embeddings to share signal across similar types; boost rare activities during sampling

**Q: How do you avoid showing repetitive feeds?**
→ Track historical recommendations in DB; filter already-seen items before scoring

**Q: How often to retrain?**
→ Monitor online CTR; trigger retraining when online metrics degrade; target multiple retrains/day

**Q: How do you handle personalization for new users (cold start)?**
→ Use demographic features + popular content; populate with connection-based signals immediately

keyQuestions: Why use Normalized Cross-Entropy instead of raw log-loss?
How do you prevent the feed from showing repetitive content?
How do you collect unbiased training data for feed ranking?
How would you handle a new user with no engagement history?
What's the tradeoff between retraining frequency and system cost?
How do you balance different activity types (posts vs. job changes) in the feed?
keyAnswers: NCE normalizes by the entropy of the label distribution, making it comparable across user segments with different base CTRs. Raw log-loss varies with background click rate, making cross-segment model comparisons misleading.

Track recent impressions per user and apply diversity constraints: limit posts from the same author or topic cluster within a sliding window. Use Determinantal Point Processes (DPP) for principled diversity post-ranking.

Use inverse propensity weighting (IPW) to correct for position bias — top-position items get more clicks regardless of relevance. Run randomized shuffling experiments on a small % of users to collect unbiased click signals.

Use demographic features and connections' engagement signals immediately. Populate feed from the user's network's recent activity; rely on content-based signals (post type, topic) rather than collaborative filtering until history accumulates.

More frequent retraining captures viral posts and trend shifts but increases compute, storage, and deployment overhead. Balance by retraining the lightweight ranking model hourly while keeping expensive candidate generation on a daily cycle.

Train separate ranking models per content type with type-specific engagement labels; apply a type-diversity constraint at re-ranking to ensure a healthy mix. Multi-task learning can share lower-layer representations across content types.

problems.title: Ad Click Prediction (Ads Ranking)
problems.description: Build an ML model that predicts whether a user will click on an ad. One of the most critical problems in online advertising — even small accuracy improvements translate to millions in revenue.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content:
**Goal**: Predict whether a user will click on a displayed advertisement. Binary classification problem:
• Input: Features describing the User (demographics, interests, history) + Ad (creative, category, budget) + Context (time, device, location, placement)
• Output: Probability P(click) ∈ [0,1]

**Business impact**: Revenue generation, user experience quality, advertiser ROI. At scale, small improvements = millions in additional revenue.

**Ad Serving — Waterfall Model**:
Publishers sell ad impressions via direct deals (highest CPM first) → if unsold, passed down to ad networks/exchanges → continues until sold. Faster and more accurate click prediction = better revenue and user experience.

**Scale**: 40K ad requests/second, 100 billion ad requests/month, 100 million users.

sections.title: Metrics Design & Requirements
sections.content:
**Offline Metrics (training phase)**:
• Normalized Cross-Entropy (NCE): Predictive log-loss divided by cross-entropy of background CTR → insensitive to background CTR. Formula: NCE = [-1/N Σ((1+yᵢ)/2·log(pᵢ) + (1-yᵢ)/2·log(1-pᵢ))] / [-(p·log(p) + (1-p)·log(1-p))]. Penalizes confident wrong predictions.
• Revenue Lift: % revenue change during A/B test on small % of traffic

**Online Metrics**: CTR improvement, Revenue per thousand impressions (RPM)

**Requirements**:
• Imbalanced data: CTR is very small (1–2%) → need techniques for highly imbalanced training
• Retraining frequency: Retrain many times/day to capture distribution shift
• Train/validation split: Partition by time to simulate production
• Serving latency: 50–100ms for ad prediction (ads go through waterfall → must be fast)
• Overspend prevention: Avoid repeatedly serving same ads → over-spending campaign budget

sections.title: Feature Engineering & Model
sections.content:
**Feature Engineering**:
| Feature | Encoding |
|---------|----------|
| AdvertiserID | Embedding or feature hashing (millions of advertisers) |
| User historical behavior (clicks over time periods) | Feature scaling (normalization) |
| Temporal features (time_of_day, day_of_week) | One-hot encoding |
| Cross features | Combine multiple features for interaction signals |

**Training Data**:
• Collect from ad click logs (waterfall model provides large volumes)
• Select training period: last month or 6 months (balance training time vs. accuracy)
• Downsample negatives to handle CTR imbalance

**Model**:
• Fully connected neural network with Sigmoid activation (final layer) — natural fit for P(click) ∈ [0,1]
• Distributed training at scale
• Evaluation: Temporal split (train on data up to time T, test on T+1); replay evaluation to avoid biased offline evaluation

sections.title: Calculation & Estimation
sections.content:
**Data Size**:
• 100B ad requests/month × 1% CTR = 1B clicked ads/month
• Each record ~500 bytes × 100B = 50 PB/month raw
• Solution: Downsample to 1–10%, or use 1 week for training + 1 day for validation

**System Components**:
• Data Lake: Collects logs/event-driven data (Kafka)
• Batch Data Prep: ETL jobs → Training Data Store
• Batch Training Jobs: Scheduled + on-demand retraining
• Model Store: S3 for trained models
• Stream Data Prep: Processes online features → key-value store (low latency)
• Feature Store: Real-time feature lookup (Redis/DynamoDB, < 10ms)
• Ad Candidates: Upstream candidate generation service (waterfall model)

sections.title: High-Level System Design & Scaling
sections.content:
**Inference Flow**:
1. User visits homepage → Ad request to Candidate Generation Service
2. Candidate Generation returns list of Ad Candidates → Aggregator Service
3. Aggregator Service splits candidate list → distributes to Ad Ranking workers (parallel scoring)
4. Ad Ranking Service: loads latest model from Model Repos + features from Feature Store → produces ad scores
5. Aggregator collects scores → selects Top-K ads (k=10–100) → returns to upstream services

**Scaling Challenge**:
• 50ms–100ms latency for 50K–100K ad candidates → single serving instance can't achieve SLA
• Solution: Aggregator Service distributes candidate list to multiple serving instances → collects and merges results

**Further Scaling**:
• Kube-flow: Ad Generation Service communicates directly with Ad Ranking Services (reduces latency)
• Load Balancer + horizontal pod autoscaling
• Separate Feature Store sharded by user/ad segments

**Follow-up Questions**:
**Q: How to adapt to changing user behavior?**
→ Retrain model every few hours with new click data

**Q: How to handle under-exploration in Ad Ranking?**
→ 2% of requests get random candidates; 98% get sorted candidates

keyQuestions: Why use Normalized Cross-Entropy instead of standard log-loss for ads?
How do you handle extreme class imbalance (1-2% CTR)?
How does the waterfall model affect your latency requirements?
How do you prevent campaign overspend?
How does the Aggregator Service pattern help achieve low latency at scale?
How would you design the feature store for real-time ad serving?
keyAnswers: Ad CTR varies widely across campaigns (0.1%–5%). NCE normalizes by background CTR so models from different campaigns are comparable and you can detect meaningful improvements rather than just reflecting base rate differences.

Downsample negatives by 10-100x during training, then re-calibrate predicted probabilities using p / (p + (1-p)/w). Alternatively apply focal loss or cost-sensitive weighting so the model focuses on hard positives.

Each stage (targeting → retrieval → ranking → auction) runs sequentially with strict time budgets. The ranking stage typically has ~50ms, ruling out expensive models like large transformers unless you apply distillation or approximation.

Use a token-bucket rate limiter per campaign. At auction time check remaining budget; apply pacing signals to probabilistically throttle ads as budget depletes. Reconcile actual spend asynchronously with the billing system.

The aggregator fans out in parallel to feature store, ad index, and user embedding service simultaneously, then assembles results. This reduces latency from O(n) serial calls to O(1) parallel, bounded by the slowest dependency.

Two-tier store: offline batch features in BigTable/Cassandra keyed by user/ad ID (read latency ~5ms); online streaming features in Redis for real-time signals (last click, current session). Combine both at serving time.

problems.title: Rental Search Ranking System (Airbnb)
problems.description: Design a machine learning system to rank rental search results on Airbnb. Return listings most likely to be booked near the top — going beyond simple keyword matching to a data-driven booking probability model.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content:
**Goal**: Rank homes in search results such that those most likely to be booked appear higher. Train a supervised ML model that learns from historical user sessions and predicts whether a listing will be booked.

**Why binary classification (booked vs. not booked)?**
• Outcome is binary → natural fit
• Flexibility to evaluate ranking, user behavior, and optimize for downstream metrics (revenue)
• More control over false positive vs. false negative tradeoffs — critical in high-stakes ranking

**Why not simple keyword matching?**
• Text similarity shows "sounds good" results but doesn't predict bookings
• Need data-driven approach: estimate P(booking | user, listing, context)

**Scale**: 150 million users, 1.25 billion searches/month, 5 bookings/user/year × 30 listings seen/booking.

sections.title: Metrics Design & Requirements
sections.content:
**Offline Metrics**:
• Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (nDCG): Standard metric for ranking where position matters. Users rarely scroll through all results → correct prediction at position 2 is more valuable than at position 10.
• Formula: nDCG = DCG / IDCG, where DCGₚ = Σᵢ relᵢ / log₂(i+1)
• Captures both relevance AND position quality

**Online Metrics**:
• Conversion rate = number_of_bookings / number_of_search_results
• Revenue lift

**Training Requirements**:
• Handle heavily imbalanced data (most listings don't get booked)
• Stratified sampling, class weighting, focal loss, or downsampling non-bookings
• Temporal split: Use sessions before cutoff date for training, after cutoff for validation → simulates production

**Inference Requirements**:
• Latency: 50–100ms — fast search is critical for user experience
• Cold-start: New listings have no data → use hybrid models or rule-based heuristics (high-quality photos, competitive pricing)

sections.title: Feature Engineering & Model
sections.content:
**Feature Categories**:
• Listing features: price, location, amenities, review scores, photos quality, availability, listing age
• User features: search history, booking history, demographics, price range preference
• Context features: search query (location, dates, guests), season, day of week
• Cross features: user-listing affinity (has user searched similar listings before?)

**Model Architecture**:
• Neural network with embeddings for categorical features (ListingID, UserID, LocationID)
• Sigmoid activation at output layer → P(booking) ∈ [0,1]
• Can extend to multi-task: predict both booking probability AND view time (if strong correlation)

**Training Data**:
• From user search sessions: each (user, listing, context) → booked or not
• 1.25B samples/month × 500 bytes = 625 GB/month
• Keep last 6–12 months; archive older data to cold storage

**Evaluation**:
• Offline: nDCG on held-out temporal validation set
• Online: Conversion rate and revenue lift via A/B test

sections.title: High-Level System Design
sections.content:
**System Flow**:
1. User searches (city + dates) → App Server → Search Service
2. Search Service queries Index DB → retrieves candidate listings
3. Candidates sent to Ranking Service → ML model scores each for P(booking)
4. Ranking Service returns scored + sorted list → Search Service → App Server → User
5. Logs all ranked candidates (for training data) → Cloud Storage or Kafka

**Key Components**:
• Feature Store (MySQL Cluster, Redis, DynamoDB): Low-latency feature lookup < 10ms
• Model Store (S3): Stores trained models; serving pulls latest by timestamp
• Feature Pipeline: Processes online features → stores in Feature Store
• Index DB: Pre-indexed listing catalog for fast retrieval

**Data Size**: 500 bytes × 1.25B samples = 625 GB/month

sections.title: Scale & Follow-Up Questions
sections.content:
**Scaling Strategy**:
• Scale out Application Servers → Load Balancers
• Scale out Search Services and Ranking Services independently (horizontal pods)
• Log all recommended candidates to Kafka → training data pipeline

**Follow-up Questions**:
**Q: What are the cons of ListingID embeddings?**
→ Limited unique IDs (millions); each listing has few bookings/year → insufficient to train good embeddings. Use listing features instead.

**Q: How to redesign for view-time correlation?**
→ Multi-task network with two output heads: P(booking) + predicted_view_time

**Q: How often to retrain?**
→ Monitor online conversion rate; trigger retraining when metrics degrade

**Q: Cold start for new listings?**
→ Use content features (photos, description, location, price) + rule-based boosting for new listings

keyQuestions: Why is nDCG a better metric than AUC for search ranking?
How do you handle the cold-start problem for new listings?
How do you prevent the model from ignoring rare but high-value bookings?
What's the tradeoff between booking probability and revenue per booking?
How would you handle temporal drift in user booking patterns (e.g., seasonal changes)?
How do you log training data without introducing position bias?
keyAnswers: nDCG measures the quality of the full ranking order — highly relevant items at the top matter more than lower positions. AUC only measures discrimination between relevant and irrelevant, ignoring rank. For search, position 1 gets ~10x more clicks than position 5.

Use content-based features available immediately (location, price, photos, amenities) to estimate booking probability. Apply a small exploration boost to new listings and collect feedback quickly to build listing-specific history.

Use value-weighted loss: multiply the loss by booking revenue or P(book) × price. Alternatively train separate models for click probability and booking probability, then combine with a revenue-aware scoring function.

Optimizing purely for booking probability favors cheap listings; optimizing for revenue favors expensive ones that convert less. Use a combined objective: P(book) × price, or a multi-objective model with explicit tradeoff control.

Add time features (month, is_holiday, days_until_travel) to the model. Retrain monthly to capture seasonal shifts. Apply recency weighting in training so recent bookings have higher loss weight than old data.

Use inverse propensity scoring: weight training examples by 1/P(shown at position k). Alternatively run position-randomized experiments on a small traffic slice to collect unbiased click signals for debiased training.

problems.title: Food Delivery ETA Prediction
problems.description: Build an ML system to estimate food delivery time given order details, market conditions, and real-time traffic. Accurate ETAs improve user trust and order completion rates.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content:
**Goal**: Build a model to estimate total delivery time given:
• Order details (order size, restaurant location, customer location)
• Market conditions (weather, events, holidays)
• Real-time traffic (road closures, congestion)

**Formula**: DeliveryTime = PickupTime + Point-to-PointTime + Drop-offTime

**Why this matters**: Overestimate → customers less likely to order. Underestimate → customers upset. Accurate ETA = trust = more orders.

**Scale**: 20 million users, 20 million deliveries/year, 300K restaurants, 200K drivers. System makes ~30 ETA predictions per delivery (updated as status changes).

sections.title: Metrics Design & Requirements
sections.content:
**Offline Metrics**:
• Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): √[Σ(predicted - actual)² / n]
• Target: RMSE < 10–15 minutes for reliable estimates
• RMSE penalizes large errors heavily — important because a 30-minute error is much worse than three 10-minute errors

**Online Metrics**:
• A/B testing: Monitor RMSE in production
• Customer engagement: Order completion rate
• Retention: Repeat order rate (long-term trust signal)

**Requirements**:
• Training: High-throughput pipeline (data in Parquet files), retrain every few hours — delivery is dynamic (traffic, weather, game days can suddenly change conditions)
• Inference: Near real-time updates whenever delivery status changes; 30 predictions per delivery; latency 100–200ms
• Balance overestimation vs. underestimation — retrain multiple times/day to adapt to market dynamics

sections.title: Feature Engineering & Model
sections.content:
**Feature Categories**:

Order features:
• Order size (number of items, weight)
• Restaurant preparation time (historical average per restaurant)
• Distance: restaurant → customer (straight-line and road distance)

Real-time features:
• Traffic speed per road segment
• Weather conditions (rain, snow, visibility)
• Active driver density in area
• Day of week, hour of day, holiday flag

Context features:
• StoreID embedding (capture restaurant-specific patterns)
• Historical average delivery time per restaurant per time-of-day

**Model**:
• Baseline: Simple rule — distance / average speed
• ML: Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDT / XGBoost) — works well with tabular features, handles missing values, interpretable
• Advanced: Segment-level model (predict speed per road segment, sum across route) → better generalization

**Training Data**:
• 2M deliveries/month × 500 bytes = 1 GB/month
• Temporal split: Train on past data, validate on most recent period

sections.title: System Design
sections.content:
**Core Services**:
• Estimate Delivery Time Service: Loads latest ML model from Model Storage, gets features from Feature Store, returns ETA prediction
• Status Service: Receives real-time updates from restaurant/driver → updates order status → publishes to Kafka
• Notification Service: Subscribed to Kafka → receives latest order status near real-time → notifies customer

**Feature Store**: DynamoDB or Redis — key-value storage for fast (<10ms) feature lookup at inference time

**Feature Pipeline**: Reads from Kafka → transforms and aggregates near real-time statistics → stores in Feature Store

**Scheduler**: Coordinates model retraining multiple times/day → saves new model to Model Storage (S3)

**System Flow**:
• Customer opens app → requests ETA → App Server → Estimate Delivery Time Service
• Service loads model + features → predicts delivery time → returns to customer
• When restaurant starts cooking → Status Service → Kafka → Notification Service updates customer
• Feature Pipeline continuously updates aggregated traffic/weather features in Feature Store

sections.title: Scaling & Follow-Up Questions
sections.content:
**Scaling**:
• Scale out Application Servers + Load Balancers
• Kafka as backbone for real-time event streaming (status updates, model predictions)
• Model predictions sent to Kafka → other services receive notifications immediately

**Follow-up Questions**:
**Q: What are the cons of StoreID embedding?**
→ New stores have no data → cold start problem. Need fallback to category-level or location-level features.

**Q: How often to retrain?**
→ Monitor online RMSE continuously; retrain when metrics degrade; target multiple retrains/day for dynamic conditions (events, weather)

**Q: How to model uncertainty in ETA?**
→ Predict distribution (mean + confidence interval) using quantile regression; show range to user ("35–45 min")

**Q: What changes for ride-sharing ETA vs. food delivery?**
→ Food delivery has 3-phase structure (prep + pickup + delivery); ride-sharing is 2-phase (pickup + trip). Food delivery needs restaurant state tracking.

keyQuestions: Why use RMSE instead of MAE for ETA prediction?
How do you handle real-time traffic vs. historical speed data?
How do you update ETA in near real-time as conditions change?
What's the cold-start problem for new restaurants and how do you solve it?
How would you model prediction uncertainty to show users a time range?
How does the feature pipeline aggregate near-real-time statistics?
keyAnswers: RMSE penalizes large errors more heavily due to squaring. In ETA, a 20-minute error is far worse than two 10-minute errors — customers who wait much longer than promised churn. RMSE's squared penalty aligns with this business cost.

Combine both: use historical average speeds by road segment and time-of-day as a prior; layer real-time GPS probe data from active couriers to detect current congestion. Weight real-time signals higher with a recency decay factor.

Push updated ETAs every 30-60 seconds per order via WebSocket. Recalculate the delivery leg from current courier GPS using a live traffic graph; recalculate the restaurant prep leg using historical prep time distribution plus real-time kitchen state signals.

New restaurants have no prep time history. Bootstrap with category-level averages (pizza ~12 min, sushi ~18 min), then add restaurant-specific features (menu complexity, kitchen capacity). Update estimates after 20-30 orders of feedback.

Use quantile regression or a Bayesian neural network to output P10/P90 confidence bounds. Show the user the 90th percentile as the 'latest' arrival time so the majority of orders beat the displayed estimate, improving trust.

Use a streaming pipeline (Kafka + Flink) to compute running aggregates: courier speed over last 5 minutes, restaurant average prep time over last hour, zone density over last 10 minutes. Materialize results to Redis for low-latency serving.

problems.title: Search Ranking System (Twitter/Google)
problems.description: Design an ML-powered search ranking system that retrieves and ranks the most relevant documents or tweets for a user's query — balancing relevance, freshness, and personalization.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content:
**Goal**: Given a user query, retrieve and rank documents/tweets so the most relevant results appear first.

**ML Formulation**: Given a query q and document d, predict a relevance score r(q,d) and rank by descending score.

**Types of ranking**:
• Pointwise: Train a model to predict relevance of each (query, document) pair independently
• Pairwise: Train model to predict which of two documents is more relevant
• Listwise: Optimize the whole ranked list directly (LambdaRank, etc.)

**Clarifying questions**:
• Is it a general search (Google) or specialized (Twitter, Amazon products)?
• What is the primary business objective? CTR, conversion, watch time?
• Personalized results or same for all users with the same query?
• How many languages need to be supported?
• What types of content — text, images, videos, mixed?

**Scale**: 50 million DAU, hundreds of millions of documents/tweets indexed.

sections.title: Metrics Design
sections.content:
**Offline Metrics**:
• NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain): Standard ranking metric — rewards relevant results at top positions
• MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank): Average of 1/rank of first relevant result
• Precision@K / Recall@K: Precision and recall in top-K results

**Online Metrics**:
• Click-Through Rate (CTR)
• Session success rate (did user find what they were looking for?)
• Dwell time (time spent on clicked result)
• Zero-click rate (user satisfied by snippet without clicking)
• Abandonment rate

**Counter Metrics**: Hide rate, report rate (user hides or reports result as irrelevant)

**Requirements**:
• Response time < 200ms end-to-end
• Freshness: New content must be indexed and rankable within minutes (Twitter) or hours (Google)
• Personalization: Same query should return different results for different users based on history

sections.title: Architectural Components & Feature Engineering
sections.content:
**Multi-Stage Architecture**:
1. Query understanding: Parse and expand query (spell correction, synonyms, intent classification)
2. Document retrieval: BM25 or ANN search → top-K candidates (fast, recall-oriented)
3. Re-ranking: ML model scores each candidate (precision-oriented)
4. Filtering: Remove spam, duplicates, age-restricted content

**Feature Engineering**:
• Query features: length, type (navigational/informational), language
• Document features: TF-IDF, BM25 score, document freshness, authority (PageRank)
• User features: search history, clicked documents, location, device
• Query-document features: query terms in title/body/URL, semantic similarity (embedding dot product)
• Context: time of day, trending topics

**Embeddings**:
• Dual-encoder: Separate encoders for query and document → dot product for relevance score
• BERT-based: Cross-encoder for precise scoring (expensive — use for re-ranking only)

sections.title: Training Data & Model
sections.content:
**Training Data Generation**:
• Click logs: (query, document, clicked) pairs from user sessions
• Dwell time as relevance signal: Short dwell = not relevant; long dwell = relevant
• Human-rated relevance (for core quality evaluation): Expert raters label query-document relevance 1–5
• Implicit feedback: clicks, bookmarks, shares as positive signals; skips as negative

**Handling Bias**:
• Position bias: Documents shown at top get more clicks even if less relevant → use Inverse Propensity Scoring (IPS)
• Selection bias: We only have feedback on shown documents → use exploration (random injection)

**Model**:
• LambdaRank / LambdaMART: Gradient-boosted trees that directly optimize NDCG
• Two-Tower Neural Network: Query encoder + Document encoder → fast at inference
• Cross-encoder (BERT): Most accurate but slow — use only in final re-ranking stage

**Training**:
• Pairwise training: For each query, train model to rank clicked docs above non-clicked docs
• Temporal split: Train on historical sessions, validate on most recent

sections.title: System Design & Scaling
sections.content:
**Indexing Pipeline**:
• Crawl/ingest new documents → extract text → compute embeddings → update ANN index
• For Twitter: Near-real-time indexing (seconds); for web: crawl frequency based on PageRank

**Serving Pipeline** (< 200ms total budget):
• Query processing: 10ms
• ANN retrieval (candidate recall): 20ms — FAISS/ScaNN vector search
• Feature extraction: 30ms — Feature Store lookup
• Re-ranking model inference: 50ms — LambdaMART or cross-encoder
• Post-processing + filtering: 20ms

**Scaling**:
• Shard index across multiple machines (by document ID or topic cluster)
• Cache popular query results with TTL-based invalidation
• Separate serving clusters for personalized vs. non-personalized results

**Follow-Up Questions**:
**Q: How do you handle freshness vs. quality tradeoff?**
→ Boost freshness score for time-sensitive queries (news, events); use decay function for document age

**Q: How do you detect and filter spam?**
→ Separate spam classifier trained on labeled spam samples; run before re-ranking

keyQuestions: What are the tradeoffs between pointwise, pairwise, and listwise ranking?
How do you handle position bias in click-through data?
When would you use a cross-encoder vs. a dual-encoder model?
How do you balance freshness vs. quality in ranking?
How do you design the indexing pipeline for near-real-time content?
How do you measure whether search quality improved in production?
keyAnswers: Pointwise is simplest but ignores relative order; pairwise (RankNet) directly optimizes pair order but is O(n²) in pairs; listwise (LambdaMART) optimizes the full list metric (nDCG) directly. Listwise gives best ranking quality at the cost of more training complexity.

Use inverse propensity weighting: assign weight 1/P(clicked | shown at position k) to each training example. Estimate propensities via randomized result-swapping experiments. Alternatively use unbiased LambdaRank which corrects gradients directly.

Dual-encoder (query and document embedded separately, scored via cosine similarity) is fast for retrieval at scale — pre-compute document embeddings offline. Cross-encoder (query+doc together through a transformer) is far more accurate but expensive — use only for re-ranking the top 100 candidates.

Add a freshness signal (document age × engagement velocity) as a ranking feature. Tune the freshness weight via A/B test on engagement metrics. Breaking news and event queries get higher freshness weight than evergreen informational queries.

Use a tiered index: a batch index rebuilt daily covering all content; a real-time index updated within seconds for new/updated content. Queries fan out to both indices and merge results. The real-time index is smaller and optimized for fast writes.

Primary: A/B test nDCG@10, CTR, and session success rate (user clicked and did not immediately return). Secondary: zero-result rate and query reformulation rate. Use interleaved testing for high sensitivity with less traffic needed.

problems.title: Fraud Detection System
problems.description: Design a real-time ML system to detect fraudulent transactions before they complete. Minimize fraud losses while keeping false positive rates low to avoid blocking legitimate transactions.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content:
**Goal**: Detect fraudulent transactions before they complete, in real-time (< 100ms).

**ML Formulation**: Binary classification — P(fraud | transaction features)

**Key constraint**: Extreme class imbalance (fraud ≈ 0.1% of all transactions) + hard latency requirement (< 100ms) + delayed labels (chargebacks arrive 30–90 days later)

**Fraud Types**:
• Account Takeover (ATO): Stolen credentials used to access accounts
• Card-Not-Present (CNP): Stolen card details used online
• Merchant Fraud: Fraudulent merchants charging customers
• Friendly Fraud: Legitimate customers dispute valid charges

**Clarifying Questions**:
• Block in real-time or flag for manual review?
• What's the acceptable false positive rate (blocking legitimate transactions has real cost)?
• Account takeover vs. card fraud vs. merchant fraud?
• How much label delay is acceptable for training?

**Scale**: 10K+ transactions/second at peak.

sections.title: Metrics Design
sections.content:
**Offline Metrics** (class imbalance makes ROC-AUC misleading):
• PR-AUC (Precision-Recall AUC): Preferred for imbalanced data — focuses on minority class
• Precision@K and Recall@K at operating threshold
• F1 score at 99% precision operating point (business requirement)

**Online Metrics**:
• Fraud rate: $ caught / $ total fraud (recall of dollar value)
• False positive rate: Legitimate transactions incorrectly blocked
• Chargeback rate and dispute rate
• Customer friction score

**Key Tradeoff**: Precision vs. Recall
• High precision = fewer false positives (less customer friction)
• High recall = more fraud caught (less financial loss)
• Business defines operating point: what's the cost of each false positive vs. false negative?

**Requirements**:
• Real-time: Decision in < 100ms
• High recall at 99%+ precision for hard fraud cases
• Monitor for model degradation as fraud patterns evolve

sections.title: Feature Engineering
sections.content:
**User/Account Features**:
• Account age, total historical spend, device fingerprint, location history
• Number of recent failed logins, password reset events
• Historical fraud flags (previous disputes)

**Transaction Features**:
• Amount, merchant category code (MCC), currency, country
• Time of day, day of week, distance from home location
• Is this merchant new for this user?

**Velocity Features** (most important for fraud detection):
• # transactions in last 1hr / 24hr / 7 days
• Total $ spent in last 1hr / 24hr
• # unique merchants in last 24hr
• # countries in last 7 days

**Graph/Network Features** (catch fraud rings):
• Shared device/IP/email across multiple accounts
• Graph distance to known fraud accounts
• Velocity of new connections between accounts

**Behavioral Biometric Features** (bot detection):
• Typing speed, mouse movement patterns, touch pressure (mobile)
• Browser fingerprint, font list, canvas fingerprint

sections.title: Model Architecture
sections.content:
**Layered Defense Strategy**:

**Layer 1 — Rules Engine (instant, < 1ms)**:
• Block obvious fraud: known bad cards, IP blacklists, velocity limits exceeded
• High precision, limited recall

**Layer 2 — ML Model (real-time, < 50ms)**:
• Gradient Boosted Trees (XGBoost/LightGBM): Fast inference, handles tabular features, interpretable
• Trained on historical labeled transactions; handles imbalance via class weights or SMOTE
• Output: risk score 0–100

**Layer 3 — Graph Neural Network (async, < 5 seconds)**:
• Detect fraud rings through connected account graph
• Flags accounts for review even if individual transaction looks clean

**Threshold Selection**:
• Set decision threshold at business-defined operating point (e.g., 99% precision)
• Different thresholds for different risk tiers (new account vs. established account)

**Handling Label Delay**:
• Chargebacks arrive 30–90 days late → use early signals: disputes, customer calls
• Semi-supervised learning with early weak labels + update with confirmed labels later

sections.title: System Design & Scaling
sections.content:
**Real-Time Serving Pipeline** (< 100ms):
• Transaction event arrives → Feature extraction from Redis (velocity counters, account features)
• Rules Engine check: block if obvious fraud
• ML model scoring: risk score computation
• Decision: block / allow / manual review queue

**Feature Serving**:
• Pre-aggregate velocity counters in Redis (e.g., tx_count_1h per user_id)
• Update counters in real-time via Kafka consumer
• Feature Store lookup: < 10ms

**Model Serving**:
• ONNX-exported model, co-located with payment service for lowest latency
• Fallback: Rules-only mode if ML service is degraded

**Offline Pipeline**:
• Collect new chargeback labels (30-90 day lag)
• Weekly model retraining with latest labeled data
• Monitor score distribution daily for concept drift

**Follow-Up Questions**:
**Q: How do you handle class imbalance?**
→ Oversample fraud (SMOTE), undersample legitimate transactions, use class weights, or focal loss

**Q: How do you detect fraud rings?**
→ Graph neural network on shared device/IP/email signals; connected components analysis

keyQuestions: Why use PR-AUC instead of ROC-AUC for fraud detection?
How do you handle the 30-90 day label delay from chargebacks?
How would you detect a coordinated fraud ring using graph features?
What's the cost of a false positive vs. false negative in fraud detection?
How do you prevent the model from learning spurious correlations in imbalanced data?
How do you handle concept drift as fraudsters adapt to your model?
keyAnswers: With extreme class imbalance (~0.1% fraud), ROC-AUC is misleadingly high because it counts true negatives. PR-AUC focuses on precision and recall for the positive (fraud) class — the metric that actually reflects business impact.

Use early proxy labels (user complaints, manual review flags) that arrive sooner than chargebacks. Train with delayed labels but upweight recent data. Run a short-term model on immediate velocity features alongside a long-term model on settled chargeback labels.

Model transactions as a graph: nodes = users/devices/IPs, edges = shared attributes (same device fingerprint, same IP subnet). Use connected components or a GNN to surface clusters of accounts with unusual shared signals that no single account shows alone.

False negative = missed fraud = direct financial loss. False positive = blocking a legitimate user = churn and support cost. Set the decision threshold to minimize total cost: FP_cost × FP_rate + FN_cost × FN_rate, calibrated from business data.

Apply stratified sampling in train/validation splits. Use SMOTE or cost-sensitive weighting for the minority class. Regularly audit feature importances — if a feature that shouldn't predict fraud (e.g., device brand) is highly weighted, investigate for data leakage.

Monitor model performance (PR-AUC, fraud dollar volume) weekly. Alert on input feature distribution shift. Retrain frequently; use adversarial training and online learning to adapt faster. Maintain a rule-based fallback layer updatable without retraining.

problems.title: Harmful Content Detection
problems.description: Design an ML system to automatically detect and remove harmful content (hate speech, violence, nudity) on a social media platform at scale — 500 million posts per day.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content:
**Goal**: Monitor posts, detect harmful content, and demote/remove it automatically.

**ML Formulation**:
• Input: Post (multimodal — text, image, video)
• Output: P(harmful) or multi-class P(violent), P(nude), P(hate_speech)
• ML Category: Multimodal Multi-label Classification

**Harmful Content Categories**:
• Violence: Graphic violence, threats
• Nudity: Adult content, CSAM
• Hate Speech: Content targeting protected groups
• Misinformation: False claims about health, elections
• Spam: Bulk unsolicited content

**Scale**: 500M posts/day, ~10K annotated examples available

**Key Constraints**:
• Latency: Can vary by category (CSAM = immediate; hate speech = can tolerate minutes)
• Explainability: Must explain the reason to affected users (which category)
• Multi-language: Must support many languages

**Clarifying Questions**:
• Which types of harmful content are in scope?
• What human annotation resources are available?
• Is there a user reporting feature (explicit feedback)?
• Is explainability required?

sections.title: Metrics Design
sections.content:
**Offline Metrics**:
• F1 score per category (harm type)
• PR-AUC (class imbalance — harmful content is rare)
• ROC-AUC

**Online Metrics**:
• Prevalence of harmful content (% of posts that are harmful)
• Appeals rate (users appealing removals — proxy for false positive rate)
• User reports rate (users reporting missed harmful content — proxy for false negative rate)
• Time to removal (for urgent categories like CSAM)

**Requirements**:
• High recall for severe categories (CSAM, terrorism) — better to over-remove
• Higher precision for borderline categories (satire, news reporting of violence)
• Latency varies: CSAM < 1 second; hate speech < 5 minutes (can batch process)
• Scale: 500M posts/day ≈ 6K posts/second

**Tradeoff**: Over-moderation (remove legitimate speech) vs. under-moderation (allow harmful content). Different content types have different optimal operating points.

sections.title: Architecture & Model
sections.content:
**Multi-Stage Architecture**:

**Stage 1 — Fast pre-filter (< 50ms)**:
• Hash-matching: Known harmful content (PhotoDNA for CSAM)
• Simple text classifiers for obvious cases
• Very high precision — only block clear violations

**Stage 2 — ML Scoring (< 500ms)**:
• Multimodal model scores text + image + video separately
• Ensemble or joint model combines signals

**Stage 3 — Human Review Queue** (for borderline cases):
• Route to human reviewers based on confidence score
• Prioritize by severity and virality

**Model Architecture**:
• Text: Fine-tuned multilingual BERT (or XLM-RoBERTa) for cross-language support
• Image: ResNet/EfficientNet fine-tuned on moderation-labeled dataset
• Video: Frame sampling (1fps) + audio transcript → apply text + image models
• Fusion: Late fusion (combine predictions) or joint model

**Training Data Challenges**:
• Only 10K annotated examples → use transfer learning from pre-trained models
• Semi-supervised: Use unlabeled data with pseudo-labels
• Active learning: Route uncertain cases to annotators first

sections.title: Training Data & Feature Engineering
sections.content:
**Labeling Methods**:
• Natural labels: User reports (noisy but free and scalable)
• Human annotation: Most accurate but expensive, slow, traumatic for annotators
• Weak supervision: Keyword lists, regex patterns, output from existing classifiers
• Transfer learning: Pre-train on large datasets, fine-tune on moderation-labeled data

**Handling Label Scarcity** (only 10K labeled examples):
• Transfer learning: Fine-tune BERT/ResNet pre-trained on large corpora
• Semi-supervised learning: Label 10K → train model → pseudo-label remaining 500M posts
• Active learning: Query most uncertain examples for human labeling first

**Feature Engineering**:
• Text: Tokenize → multilingual embeddings (XLM-RoBERTa)
• Image: CNN features + object detection labels
• Video: Frame embeddings + audio transcript text features
• User features: Account age, history of violations, follower count
• Content metadata: virality score, language, region

sections.title: System Design & Scaling
sections.content:
**Real-Time Pipeline** (for high-severity content):
• Post created → publish to Kafka topic
• Content Moderation Service consumes event → runs hash check + fast classifier
• If confident violation → auto-remove + notify user
• If uncertain → ML scoring pipeline → score-based routing

**Batch Pipeline** (for lower-severity):
• Process posts in micro-batches (every 1–5 minutes)
• Run full multimodal model scoring
• Apply thresholds per category

**Human Review Queue**:
• Priority queue based on severity × virality
• Reviewer tools: side-by-side context, policy guidelines, appeal tracking

**Scaling**:
• 6K posts/second requires distributed Kafka consumer pods
• Model serving: GPU pods for image/video inference; CPU pods for text
• Cache hash-match results to avoid re-processing known content

**Follow-Up Questions**:
**Q: How do you handle adversarial users who slightly modify harmful content to evade detection?**
→ Perceptual hashing (robust to minor modifications); adversarial training; regular model updates

**Q: How do you avoid over-moderating in culturally specific contexts?**
→ Region-specific classifiers; diverse human review teams; appeals process with human escalation

keyQuestions: How do you handle multi-modal content (text + image + video) in a unified model?
How do you handle the label scarcity problem with only 10K annotated examples?
What's the tradeoff between precision and recall for different harm categories?
How do you support content moderation across 50+ languages?
How do you handle adversarial users trying to evade detection?
How do you prioritize which content to review first when overloaded?
keyAnswers: Train modality-specific encoders (text transformer, image ViT, video frame sampler) and fuse embeddings via late fusion — concatenation followed by an MLP classifier. Each modality can independently flag content; ensemble or max-pool their scores.

Fine-tune a pre-trained foundation model (CLIP for images, BERT/RoBERTa for text) on your 10K examples. Apply data augmentation (back-translation for text, transforms for images). Use active learning to annotate the most uncertain examples first.

Child safety: maximize recall — miss nothing, even if it means more false positives going to human review. Spam: maximize precision — less user disruption even if some spam slips through. Tune the decision threshold per harm category based on severity of harm.

Use multilingual pre-trained models (mBERT, XLM-R) that share representations across languages via cross-lingual transfer. For low-resource languages, transfer from similar high-resource languages. Hire diverse annotators covering all major language communities.

Use adversarial training: augment training data with known evasion patterns (character substitution, leet speak, unicode lookalikes). Run red-team exercises to generate new evasion attempts. Monitor for sudden distribution shifts on model inputs as an early evasion signal.

Score items by risk: harmful probability × viral reach × content recency. Route highest-risk items to expedited human review. Allocate reviewers by language and regional expertise. Use tiered automated actions for high-confidence cases, reserving human review for borderline decisions.

problems.title: People You May Know (Friend Recommendation)
problems.description: Design a friend/follower recommendation system that suggests users to connect with on a social platform — similar to LinkedIn's PYMK or Facebook's Friend Suggestions.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content:
**Goal**: Recommend a list of users that you may want to connect with, maximizing the number of formed connections.

**ML Formulation**: For a given user U, rank all other users by P(U connects with V).
• Input: Source user U, candidate user V, graph context
• Output: Connection probability P(U→V)

**Connection Types**:
• Symmetric (Facebook friends): Both users must accept
• Asymmetric (Twitter follows): One-way, no acceptance needed

**Scale**: 1B total users, avg 10,000 connections per user → dense social graph

**Clarifying Questions**:
• Are friendships/connections symmetrical?
• Can we assume the social graph is relatively stable?
• What engagement counts as a "connection" (accepted request, first message, follow)?
• How many recommendations to show per user? (Typically 10–20)

**Business Goal**: Maximize formed connections (increases engagement, retention, network value)

sections.title: Metrics Design
sections.content:
**Offline Metrics**:
• Precision@K: Of K recommendations shown, what % did user connect with?
• Recall@K: Of all people user will ever connect with, what % were in top-K recommendations?
• AUC: Overall ranking quality

**Online Metrics**:
• Connection acceptance rate: # accepted requests / # recommendations shown
• Connection formation rate: New connections per DAU (primary business metric)
• Long-term engagement: Do new connections lead to ongoing interactions?
• Spam rate: Are recommendations being used to spam users?

**Requirements**:
• Freshness: New users should appear in recommendations quickly (cold-start)
• Diversity: Not just mutual friends — recommend people from different contexts
• Privacy: Some users block specific people → filter out before recommendation
• Scale: 1B users × 20 recommendations = 20B predictions/day (need efficient batching)

sections.title: Feature Engineering
sections.content:
**Graph-Based Features** (most powerful signals):
• Mutual friends count: # friends both U and V share
• Mutual friends / total friends ratio
• Graph distance: 2nd-degree connections? 3rd-degree?
• Common group memberships
• Common workplace/school affiliations

**Profile Similarity Features**:
• Hometown / current city match
• Employer / school match
• Industry / function match (LinkedIn)
• Shared interests (liked pages, groups)

**Behavioral Features**:
• Has U viewed V's profile? (strong positive signal)
• Do U and V interact with the same content?
• Are U and V in the same photo together?
• Email/phone contact book overlap

**User Features (for each candidate)**:
• User activity level (DAU? last_login)
• Profile completeness
• Response rate to connection requests

sections.title: Model Architecture
sections.content:
**Candidate Generation** (fast recall stage):
• Triangle closing: Find users with many mutual friends (graph traversal, O(k²) per user)
• Collaborative filtering: Find users with similar "taste" in connections (ALS matrix factorization)
• Inverted index on profile attributes: Same school/employer → candidate pool

**Ranking Model** (precision stage):
• Logistic Regression baseline: Fast, interpretable, good with sparse features
• Gradient Boosted Trees (XGBoost): Better for non-linear feature interactions
• Graph Neural Network: Learns embeddings by aggregating neighborhood information — captures complex graph structure

**GNN Approach**:
• Each user = node; connections = edges
• Node features: profile attributes + behavioral history
• Message passing: Aggregate features from neighbors
• Output: User embedding → dot product for connection probability

**Training Data**:
• Positive: Pairs (U, V) where connection formed within 30 days after recommendation
• Negative: Random pairs + hard negatives (recommended but not connected)
• Temporal split: Train on older data, validate on most recent

sections.title: System Design & Scaling
sections.content:
**Offline Pre-computation** (daily batch):
• For each user, run graph traversal to find candidate pool (2nd/3rd degree connections)
• Compute similarity features for all (user, candidate) pairs
• Score all pairs → rank → store top-50 recommendations per user in cache

**Online Serving** (< 100ms):
• User opens PYMK page → look up pre-computed recommendations from cache
• Apply real-time filters (blocked users, existing connections, privacy settings)
• Return top-K recommendations

**Feature Store**: Pre-computed profile features + behavioral signals for fast lookup

**Scaling Challenges**:
• 1B users × candidate generation = compute-intensive → batch with Spark/MapReduce
• Social graph changes continuously → incremental graph updates vs. full daily recompute
• Cold-start for new users: Use profile features + email/phone contact matching immediately

**Follow-Up Questions**:
**Q: How do you handle the cold-start problem for new users?**
→ Immediately match against email/phone contacts; use profile similarity (school, workplace); boost recent joiners who share attributes

**Q: How do you avoid filter bubbles (always recommending same demographic)?**
→ Add diversity constraint: Ensure recommendations span different shared-friend clusters; inject serendipitous recommendations

keyQuestions: What are the most predictive features for friend recommendation?
How do you efficiently find 2nd-degree connections at 1B user scale?
When would you use a Graph Neural Network vs. a simpler model?
How do you handle the cold-start problem for brand new users?
How do you prevent PYMK from being used for spam or harassment?
How do you balance relevance vs. diversity in friend recommendations?
keyAnswers: Mutual friends count is the strongest predictor, followed by shared workplace or school, geographic proximity, common interests or groups, recent profile views (intent signal), and frequency of co-mention in conversations.

Pre-compute 2nd-degree connection lists using bounded-depth BFS on a distributed graph store (like Unicorn at Facebook). At query time look up pre-computed neighbors, then score and rank — avoids real-time graph traversal at billion-user scale.

Use a GNN when graph structure is complex, long-range relationships matter, or you want to capture community structure. Use gradient-boosted trees when latency is critical, features are well-understood, or GNN training cost is prohibitive.

Immediately import contacts from email or phone to find existing users. Use profile attributes (school, employer, location) to surface similar users. Fall back to trending people in the user's city or industry.

Filter recommendations for accounts with abuse reports or low trust scores. Limit the number of PYMK suggestions an account can receive or generate per day. Apply anomaly detection on unusual bulk-connection attempt patterns.

Primary sort by predicted connection probability; apply post-ranking diversity rules — cap suggestions from the same workplace or school at 3, include at least one from a different city. Use a greedy diversity algorithm or DPP for principled selection.

problems.title: Hate Speech Detection System
problems.description: Design a system that ingests user-generated text and determines whether it violates hate speech policies — deciding whether to allow, remove, or escalate for human review. Accurately, fairly, and at scale.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content:
**Goal**: Ingest user-generated text → determine if it violates hate speech policies → allow / remove / escalate for human review.

**What counts as hate speech**: Content targeting protected groups (race, religion, gender, ethnicity, nationality) with derogatory, threatening, or dehumanizing language.

**Different from**:
• Offensive language (profane but not targeting a group)
• Harassment (targeting individuals, not groups)
• Sarcasm, humor, quoting for criticism/reporting, reclaimed language

**Why it's hard**:
• **Context changes meaning**: "Go back to where you came from" is neutral in travel discussion, hateful when directed at an immigrant during a heated exchange
• **Labels are subjective**: Even trained moderators regularly disagree on borderline cases; inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa) < 0.6 is common
• **Language evolves**: Users adapt to moderation with euphemisms, coded language, emojis, misspellings
• **Scale**: 500M+ posts/day

**Clarifying Questions**:
• Which specific harm categories are in scope (hate speech vs. harassment vs. violence)?
• Multi-language support required?
• What's the acceptable false positive rate?
• Is explainability required when removing content?

sections.title: Metrics Design
sections.content:
**Offline Metrics**:
• **F1 score** per category (hate type): balances precision and recall for imbalanced data
• **PR-AUC**: Better than ROC-AUC when harmful content is rare (< 1% of posts)
• **Inter-annotator agreement**: Measures label quality — if humans disagree, model can't exceed human performance
• **Calibration**: P(hate speech) should be well-calibrated for threshold selection

**Online Metrics**:
• **Prevalence**: % of posts classified as hate speech (monitor for sudden spikes/drops)
• **Appeals rate**: Users appealing removals → proxy for false positive rate (over-moderation)
• **User reports rate**: Users reporting missed content → proxy for false negative rate
• **Time to removal**: For urgent categories (CSAM, incitement to violence)

**Key Tradeoffs**:
• High recall = miss less hate speech, but over-moderate legitimate speech
• High precision = fewer false positives, but miss more violations
• Operating point varies by category: CSAM → maximize recall; satire/borderline → maximize precision

sections.title: Architectural Components
sections.content:
**Multi-Stage Decision System**:

**Stage 1 — Fast Pre-filter (< 5ms)**:
• Hash matching against known violations (perceptual/exact hashing)
• Keyword/regex blocklist for extreme content
• Automatically removes clear violations with high precision

**Stage 2 — ML Scoring (< 200ms)**:
• Contextual classifier on full post text + metadata
• Multi-label output: P(hate_speech), P(violence), P(harassment)
• Uses conversation context (prior messages, reply structure)

**Stage 3 — Human Review Queue**:
• Borderline content (model confidence 0.4–0.7) → priority queue
• Ordered by: severity × virality score
• Human decision becomes new training label

**Stage 4 — Post-Decision Actions**:
• Remove: Delete content, notify user with policy violation reason
• Demote: Reduce distribution (not shown in feeds/search)
• Label: Apply warning label (user clicks to see)
• Escalate: Refer to law enforcement (CSAM, credible violence threats)

sections.title: Model Development
sections.content:
**Model Architecture**:
• **Fine-tuned multilingual BERT** (or XLM-RoBERTa for 100+ languages): Contextual embeddings capture nuance, sarcasm, coded language better than bag-of-words
• **Input**: Full post text + optional: author history snippet, reply context, hashtags
• **Output**: Multi-label classification (not mutually exclusive harm types)

**Handling Label Subjectivity**:
• Collect multiple annotations per sample → model probability as label distribution, not single label
• Weight annotations by annotator reliability (consistency with policy)
• Use soft labels (0.7 hate, 0.3 not hate) rather than hard binary labels for borderline cases

**Training Data Challenges**:
• **Label noise**: Annotator disagreement is inherent → use confidence-weighted training
• **Data sparsity**: Extreme categories (CSAM) have few examples → oversampling, augmentation
• **Active learning**: Route low-confidence model predictions to annotators first

**Adversarial Robustness**:
• Users modify harmful content: typos, emojis, leet-speak ("h8", "🖕")
• Solution: Adversarial training with perturbed examples; character-level features; robust tokenization

sections.title: System Design & Scaling
sections.content:
**Real-Time Pipeline** (for post creation):
• Post event → Kafka topic → Content Moderation Consumer
• Fast pre-filter (hash match + keyword) → if match: auto-remove
• If uncertain → ML scoring service → confidence-based routing
• High confidence violation → auto-remove + log
• Low confidence → human review queue

**Batch Pipeline** (for existing content):
• Retroactively scan older posts when policy updates
• Re-score posts after model improvement

**Feature Store**:
• User history features: prior violations, account age, follower count
• Content features: embedding cache for repeated content
• Pre-computed: author risk scores updated hourly

**Scaling**:
• 500M posts/day ≈ 6K posts/second
• Kafka consumer pods auto-scaled by Kubernetes
• GPU pods for BERT inference; CPU pods for pre-filter
• Cache repeated content (memes spread virally → same content millions of times)

**Follow-Up Questions**:
**Q: How do you handle language evolution / adversarial users?**
→ Regular model retraining with new examples; character-level features; perceptual hashing

**Q: How do you ensure fairness across demographic groups?**
→ Audit false positive/negative rates by demographic; test for disparate impact; diverse annotator pools

keyQuestions: Why is hate speech detection harder than spam detection?
How do you handle subjective labels where annotators disagree?
How do you detect coded language and evolving slang that bypasses keyword filters?
What's the tradeoff between over-moderation and under-moderation?
How do you ensure fairness — not over-moderating specific communities?
How does the human review queue get prioritized at scale?
keyAnswers: Hate speech requires cultural and contextual understanding — the same phrase can be reclaimed language or a slur depending on who says it and to whom. Spam has clearer structural patterns. Hate speech is also highly language- and community-specific, requiring specialized annotators.

Use multiple annotators per item (3-5); apply majority vote for clear cases. For borderline items use a soft label (average annotator score) rather than forcing binary ground truth. Train with label smoothing or probabilistic labels to capture genuine uncertainty.

Use embedding-based models that capture semantic similarity even for novel words not seen in training. Monitor new keyword clusters appearing in flagged content. Apply active learning to quickly annotate and retrain on newly detected coded terms and evolving slang.

Over-moderation silences legitimate speech and disproportionately harms marginalized communities who use reclaimed language. Under-moderation allows real harm and drives away other users. Balance with tiered actions: remove amplification first, then content, using human review for edge cases.

Audit false positive rates by demographic group using representative held-out test sets. If a community is disproportionately flagged, investigate training data bias. Use diverse annotator pools that include members of affected communities to catch systematic blind spots.

Score items by: P(violation) × expected reach (shares, follower count) × content recency. Allocate reviewers by language and regional expertise. Use a tiered system: automated action on high-confidence cases, human expedited queue for viral content, standard queue for everything else.

problems.title: Dynamic Pricing Engine
problems.description: Design an ML system that automatically determines optimal prices in real time, responding to demand, inventory, competition, and market conditions — used by Uber Surge, Amazon, airlines, and hotels.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content:
**Goal**: Determine the optimal price at the right time by responding to: demand fluctuations, inventory levels, user behavior, seasonality, competitor prices, and business constraints.

**This is NOT just a prediction task**: ML models estimate demand or price elasticity, but actual pricing decisions are shaped by optimization logic, policy rules, and safety constraints. The model never outputs a price directly — it outputs signals (demand curves, elasticity estimates), and pricing logic happens downstream.

**Key Insight — Feedback Loop Problem**: Every price change influences customer behavior → which affects the data the system learns from. If you raise prices and sales decline, the signal is ambiguous: natural demand drop, or price sensitivity? Without careful design, the system may reinforce bad decisions.

**Business Objectives** (vary by industry):
• Ride-hailing (Uber): Balance driver supply and rider demand (Surge Pricing)
• E-commerce (Amazon): Maximize revenue or clear inventory
• Airlines/Hotels: Yield management — maximize revenue across time

**Clarifying Questions**:
• What's the primary objective — revenue, profit, market share, or demand smoothing?
• Is it real-time (ride-hailing) or batch (airline tickets)?
• Are there regulatory or fairness constraints on pricing?
• Can we A/B test price changes directly?

sections.title: Metrics Design
sections.content:
**Offline Metrics**:
• **MAPE** (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) on demand forecasts
• **Revenue per unit** at different price points in holdout experiments
• **Price elasticity accuracy**: How well does predicted elasticity match observed response?

**Online Metrics** (primary):
• **Revenue lift**: % revenue increase vs. control group (A/B test)
• **Conversion rate**: Orders / Price shows (demand response)
• **Supply utilization**: For ride-hailing — % of drivers with active trips
• **Customer satisfaction**: NPS, repeat purchase rate (long-term trust signal)

**Counter Metrics** (watch for harm):
• Churn rate: Are high prices driving users to competitors?
• Complaint rate: Perception of unfairness after price spikes
• Driver/seller satisfaction: Supply side metrics

**Key Tradeoffs**:
• Short-term revenue vs. long-term customer trust
• Exploration (test new prices to learn elasticity) vs. exploitation (use best known price)
• Local optimization (maximize this session) vs. global (maximize lifetime value)

sections.title: Feature Engineering & Model
sections.content:
**Demand Estimation Features**:
• **Temporal**: Hour of day, day of week, holiday flags, special events (concerts, sports games)
• **Supply**: Number of available drivers/rooms/inventory within radius
• **Historical demand**: Rolling average demand for same time/location over past weeks
• **External**: Weather, competitor prices (if available), local event calendars
• **User features**: User segment (price-sensitive vs. price-insensitive), loyalty tier

**Price Elasticity Features**:
• Historical response to past price changes at similar conditions
• User-level elasticity (loyal users less sensitive than new users)

**Model Architecture**:
• **Demand forecasting**: Gradient Boosted Trees (GBDT) or LSTM for time-series demand prediction
• **Price optimization**: Given demand forecast + inventory + constraints → optimization problem (not pure ML): argmax_price [revenue(price, demand(price))]
• **Causal inference**: Use diff-in-diff or instrumental variables to estimate true price elasticity (avoid confounding from self-selection)

**Multi-Armed Bandit**:
• Explore different prices on small % of traffic to continuously learn elasticity
• Thompson Sampling or UCB1 for price exploration within allowed range

sections.title: System Design
sections.content:
**Real-Time Pricing Pipeline** (Uber Surge model, < 500ms):
1. Demand signals arrive (ride requests, cancellations) → Kafka stream
2. Demand Aggregation Service: Compute supply/demand ratio per geohex cell (5-min rolling window)
3. Surge Model: Given supply/demand ratio → predict surge multiplier
4. Pricing Rules Engine: Apply caps (max 10x), fairness rules, regulatory limits
5. Price returned to app in < 500ms

**Batch Pricing Pipeline** (Airlines/Hotels, daily/hourly):
1. Pull historical booking data + competitor prices
2. Run demand forecast model for each date/segment
3. Optimize price across inventory (yield management)
4. Update price catalog
5. Monitor conversion rates hourly

**Guardrails** (critical for trust):
• Hard price caps (never exceed X × baseline)
• Ramp limits (max % change per hour)
• Fairness rules (same price for same query regardless of user history)
• Regulatory compliance (price gouging laws during emergencies)

**Experimentation**:
• A/B test: Random % of requests get experimental prices → measure revenue and conversion
• Holdout group: Never gets price changes → long-term baseline comparison

sections.title: Scaling & Follow-Up Questions
sections.content:
**Scaling**:
• Real-time: Kafka consumer pods + in-memory demand aggregation (Redis)
• Geospatial sharding: Partition by geographic region for Uber/Airbnb
• Cache surge multipliers (refresh every 30 seconds) to avoid recalculating per request

**Follow-Up Questions**:
**Q: How do you prevent the feedback loop from distorting training data?**
→ Use causal inference methods (instrumental variables); hold out control groups that never get price changes; track logging of both the offered price and the "would have been" baseline price

**Q: How do you handle the cold-start problem for new markets?**
→ Use rule-based pricing initially; transfer elasticity estimates from similar markets; bootstrap with aggressive exploration (multi-armed bandit)

**Q: How do you balance surge pricing with customer fairness perception?**
→ Communicate the reason for surge; apply caps; offer alternatives (schedule later at lower price); consider opt-out for loyal users

**Q: How do you prevent competitors from gaming your pricing model?**
→ Add noise to exploration; don't make pricing algorithm fully transparent; monitor for suspicious demand patterns (fake requests to trigger surge)

**Q: Why isn't dynamic pricing just a regression problem?**
→ Because the action (setting price) changes the outcome (demand) — it's a causal/decision problem, not a passive prediction problem

keyQuestions: Why is dynamic pricing a causal inference problem, not just a prediction problem?
How do you measure true price elasticity when prices and demand are correlated?
How do you prevent the feedback loop from corrupting your training data?
How do you balance short-term revenue vs. long-term customer trust?
How does Uber's surge pricing algorithm work at the geo-cell level?
What guardrails do you put in place to prevent price gouging?
keyAnswers: Setting a price changes demand — it's a causal decision, not a passive prediction. If you train on historical price-demand data, you get confounded estimates (prices and demand are correlated). You need causal methods (randomized experiments, instrumental variables) to isolate the true effect of price changes.
